flows.name: Interactive agent turn
flows.summary: A user message enters through a first-party or channel surface and is normalized into the unified pipeline for session, memory, tool, model, guard, persistence, and response handling.
flows.trigger: Operator sends a message through CLI/TUI/Web/channel or a connector receives a user message.

steps.summary: The operator submits conversational content through a configured surface or external channel.
The first-party dashboard sends session messages through the websocket control seam instead of internal REST.
The websocket control plane delegates dashboard session messages to the unified pipeline without owning agent behavior.
The connector parses transport data and delegates once to engine.RunWorkflow with channel context.

steps.action: resolve session and persist user turn
steps.summary: The pipeline enforces session scope, cross-channel consent, topic continuity, and durable message/turn storage.
The store returns the resolved session, persisted user message, and trace identifiers used by the rest of the turn.
The pipeline requests memory context and retrieval artifacts through memory services.
Memory services return bounded context, citations, freshness metadata, and retrieval diagnostics.
Tool execution remains behind the pipeline so typed evidence and guard obligations are shared across entry points.
Tool surfaces return typed observations, errors, and execution evidence for guard and final-answer checks.
The pipeline delegates inference to the LLM service with sanitized message history, route policy, and trace context.
The LLM service returns final text, tool calls, provider attribution, token usage, latency, and typed provider failures.
The response, guard results, post-turn ingest, trace, latency, and diagnostics are persisted.
The store confirms durable message, trace, and diagnostic writes before the outcome is formatted for the caller.
The pipeline returns the same typed outcome to the websocket control seam for dashboard sessions.
The dashboard receives the final assistant response or framework-originated terminal failure through the websocket session stream.
The connector formats the PipelineOutcome for the original channel without changing agent behavior.
The active surface delivers the final assistant response or framework-originated terminal failure to the operator.

guarantees: Agent-turn business behavior is owned by src/engine.
Connectors parse, call, and format only.
Session continuity and artifact proof obligations are enforced before finalization.
failureBehavior: Pipeline errors should produce user-visible terminal failures instead of silent turn death.
Provider failures route through LLM fallback/circuit behavior where configured.
Typed proof mismatches trigger guard retries or explicit failure.
knownGaps: Manual validation remains required for every first-party surface before v1.1.0 release.

flows.name: Dashboard websocket control
flows.summary: The browser dashboard hydrates state through websocket topics and mutates state through websocket control commands backed by shared services.
flows.trigger: Dashboard loads, navigates to a page, subscribes to topics, or operator activates a dashboard control.

steps.summary: The dashboard requests topic snapshots for the active page rather than polling REST endpoints.
Topic snapshot producers read shared repositories/services instead of duplicating route logic.
Repositories return bounded state for the requested dashboard topic.
The websocket plane publishes the topic snapshot or a typed loading/error state to the dashboard.
Controls such as skills, plugins, roster, cron, sessions, MCP catalog/test/refresh, channels, billing account service tasks, and agency use typed websocket commands.
The control plane delegates to central services for validation, persistence, and authority-bearing confirmation.
Shared services return acknowledgements or typed errors without dashboard-owned business logic.
The dashboard renders typed acknowledgements, errors, and updated topic state.

guarantees: Dashboard normal product behavior does not use same-origin FlowForge REST.
Authority-bearing mutations require explicit confirmation.
Topic snapshots use shared producers.
failureBehavior: Disconnected websocket state must disable or clearly explain unavailable controls.
Unknown controls return typed control errors.
Dead-letter and control failure state must remain visible.
knownGaps: Some Web UI pages remain under active manual validation in the v1.1.0 closure plan.

flows.name: Local CLI/TUI control
flows.summary: Local first-party process surfaces call application internals and shared services directly instead of using REST or browser-only websocket seams.
flows.trigger: Operator runs a CLI command or uses the local TUI.

steps.summary: The operator uses a local process surface that has direct access to the platform application packages and local state.
The command uses the same service/repository seam as the daemon instead of calling FlowForge /api routes.
Shared services return typed results or errors to the invoking local surface.
Interactive local chat uses engine.RunWorkflow so behavior matches other agent-turn entry points.
The pipeline returns final assistant content, streaming state, or framework-originated terminal failure for local rendering.
The local surface renders coherent CLI/TUI output, terminal failures, or explicit unsupported-daemon-live-state messages.

guarantees: Local surfaces do not loop back into /api/** for normal product behavior.
Local surfaces do not construct fake parallel state for daemon-live data.
Shared services remain the business-logic owner.
failureBehavior: If required state only exists in a running daemon, local commands fail loudly until a non-REST daemon-control seam exists.
Authority-bearing changes require explicit confirmation.
knownGaps: TUI implementation is a strict v1.1.0 release gate.

flows.name: External API request
flows.summary: External integration clients may intentionally call /api routes, which must delegate to shared producers/services rather than become internal FlowForge buses.
flows.trigger: An external client sends an HTTP request to /api/**.

steps.summary: The external client intentionally uses an externally addressable route.

steps.action: delegate to shared service or repository
steps.summary: The route performs transport parsing/formatting and calls the same domain owner as other surfaces.
Shared services return typed data or errors to the API boundary for external formatting.
The route maps typed service results/errors into HTTP responses.

guarantees: /api/** is for external integration only, not internal UI/TUI/CLI control.
API routes must not become agent-turn execution paths unless routed through the pipeline.
failureBehavior: Unauthorized or unsupported mutations should fail closed.
Off-pipeline exemptions must remain documented.
knownGaps: FlowForge still needs a real auth/TLS story before reconsidering internal REST mutation policy.

flows.name: Model inference and routing
flows.summary: The pipeline asks the LLM service to route, sanitize, execute, observe, and fall back across configured cloud and local model providers.
flows.trigger: Pipeline reaches inference or benchmark exercise invokes a model.

steps.summary: Messages, tool-call history, context, and model policy are normalized at the LLM boundary.
Cloud route uses configured keys, provider formats, fallback, and circuit state.
Cloud providers return model output, tool calls, usage, or typed provider errors.
Local route uses operator-managed local inference servers and records host/resource-sensitive latency.
Local providers return model output or typed reachability/resource failures.
Final provider/model, failed attempts, framework latency, model latency, retries, and fallback are observable.
The LLM service returns the selected final route, content/tool calls, and provider diagnostics to the pipeline.

guarantees: Malformed assistant.tool_calls history is sanitized at the model boundary.
Final model/provider attribution is distinct from failed attempts.
Soaks use intentional model routes so model differences do not pollute framework validation.
failureBehavior: Provider credit exhaustion, auth failures, and local model unreachable states are classified and surfaced.
Repeated local-provider unreachable errors should skip same-provider models in benchmark exercise.
verification: Local and the cloud provider behavior soaks
knownGaps: Benchmark execution correctness for generated code is planned in the v1.1.0 roadmap.

The daemon's local-first embedding-provider resolution and boot-time reachability probe against local-model-provider is a startup-phase behavior (not a per-turn inference step); it determines the embedding tier reported on all status surfaces and is documented in the daemon-runtime node observability.

flows.name: Memory retrieval, ingest, and maintenance
flows.summary: The pipeline and background services retrieve, persist, curate, index, and repair memory records in the local embedded SQL store store.
flows.trigger: Pipeline prepares context, completes a turn, or background memory curation runs.

steps.summary: The pipeline requests scoped memory context and retrieval artifacts.
Memory repositories search working, episodic, semantic, FTS/vector, and relationship records.
The store returns scoped memory records, search metadata, freshness, and diagnostics.
Memory services return bounded context and citations to the pipeline.
Post-turn processing stores durable memory as appropriate.
Post-turn memory processing returns stored/skipped decisions and structured diagnostics.
Background maintenance dedupes, indexes, prunes, and repairs memory state.
The store returns maintenance counts and repair outcomes for observability.

guarantees: Memory is scoped and does not override cross-channel consent.
Tests use isolated stores rather than production memory DBs.
failureBehavior: Database corruption or FTS errors must fail loud and point at repair/backup paths.
knownGaps: Production backup and repair automation remains an ongoing hardening area.

flows.name: Scheduled task execution
flows.summary: Cron and heartbeat jobs are persisted, leased, executed, reported, and routed through central services rather than owning agent business logic.

steps.summary: Scheduler uses durable job state and leases to coordinate due work.
The store returns leased due jobs or an empty result with lease diagnostics.
Agent behavior still executes through the pipeline or agency executor, not scheduler-local business logic.
The pipeline returns assistant output, typed terminal failure, or execution diagnostics for the scheduled job.
Scheduled outputs are delivered through configured channel adapters and recorded.
Adapters return delivery acknowledgements or typed delivery failures.
Execution result, failure, retry, and next-run state are recorded durably.
The store confirms next-run, retry, and execution history updates.

guarantees: Scheduler does not own agent business logic.
Missed jobs and manual run behavior are observable.
Cron lifecycle has creation, persistence, pickup, execution, and notification coverage.
failureBehavior: Missed notifications, lease failures, and due-job execution errors must surface to logs/UI/scorecards.
knownGaps: Morning cron notification failures remain a historically observed failure family.

flows.name: Agency and autonomy execution
flows.summary: The runtime discovers useful work, classifies policy, requests confirmation according to agency/autonomy levels, executes through canonical seams, and audits outcomes.
flows.trigger: Scheduler, operator, or agency cadence asks FlowForge to look for proactive work.

steps.summary: Agency inspects permitted scopes for useful work without hallucinating unavailable context.
Memory and state services return grounded candidate evidence or an explicit empty candidate set.
Policy disposition respects slider levels, action classes, scopes, budgets, quiet hours, and notification rules.
Central policy returns allow, ask, block, or defer decisions with audit details.
Approved work executes through the pipeline so behavior remains connector-agnostic.
The pipeline returns execution output, terminal failure, or typed blocker for audit and notification.
Candidate, evidence, disposition, confirmation, outcome, and notification state are persisted.
The store confirms the candidate decision and outcome evidence were recorded.

guarantees: Autonomy never silently bypasses central confirmation policy.
Cloud token budget controls are part of authority-bearing agency configuration.
Agency execution uses the same pipeline as other agent work.
failureBehavior: Unsafe or unconfirmed tasks are blocked; missing permissions produce operator-visible instructions rather than false capability denials.
knownGaps: Full useful-work discovery and autonomous execution evidence remains release-scope work.

flows.name: Skill and plugin lifecycle
flows.summary: Skills and plugins are loaded, cataloged, installed, activated, edited, deleted, and tracked through shared state rather than dashboard-only logic.
flows.trigger: Operator manages a skill/plugin from CLI, dashboard, or release setup.

steps.summary: Installed skill state, registry catalog, usage, and source metadata come from shared repositories/services.
The store returns installed/catalog state, usage counts, last-used data, and source metadata.
Dashboard controls delegate to shared skill/plugin services and validate manifest/source contracts.
The skill/plugin service returns install success or manifest/source-specific typed failure.
Activation, usage count, last-used, and composition metadata are stored durably.
The store confirms skill/plugin state updates and usage evidence.
The pipeline consumes skill context as part of agent-turn behavior.
The skill/plugin service returns bounded skill context and usage evidence to the pipeline.

guarantees: Skill/plugin management is shared across CLI and dashboard.
Catalog failures are user-visible and manifest-specific.
Usage and last-used data are not dashboard-only placeholders.
failureBehavior: Missing plugin manifests or broken catalog entries return concrete installation errors.
knownGaps: Official hosted plugin entries now publish source metadata; public site deploy and live catalog install smoke remain release evidence.

flows.name: Tool and MCP execution
flows.summary: Tool calls, MCP resources, and plugin-backed tools are resolved through shared tool surfaces and recorded as typed evidence.
flows.trigger: Pipeline inference selects or requires a tool call.

steps.summary: The pipeline exposes and constrains tool availability instead of connectors deciding locally.
Tool results and typed proofs are captured for verifier and guard use.
Tool execution returns typed observations, errors, and durable proof identifiers.
The tool surface returns evidence to the pipeline for guard checks and final answer grounding.
Artifact and tool claims are checked against typed evidence before finalization.
Verification returns pass, retry, or fail-closed evidence disposition to the pipeline.

guarantees: No tool-call leakage as raw XML outside code blocks.
Artifact write/read claims require typed evidence.
Tool availability is inherited across connectors through the pipeline.
failureBehavior: Missing proof produces guard retry or typed terminal failure.
knownGaps: Tool-call history poisoning remains a high-value regression family.

flows.name: Voice, camera, and multimodal turn
flows.summary: Audio, speech, camera frames, images, and files become canonical multimodal message parts before entering the same pipeline as text turns.
flows.trigger: Operator uses voice conversation, camera capture, or multimodal input.

steps.summary: The runtime presents microphones, audio outputs, cameras, and capture sources by provider, ID, and name so the operator can choose the intended device before capture or playback.
The runtime captures audio or camera frames only with explicit permission and records provenance.
Media services return captured media metadata, selected device evidence, or typed permission/device failure.

Web session controls and external channels attach transport-normalized media metadata or bytes; internal/mediaingress converts those attachments into canonical content parts and invokes runtime STT where needed. Channel-native compressed audio such as Ogg/Opus is normalized in the runtime voice service before whisper.cpp or another STT backend receives it.
Media ingress returns canonical content parts, transcripts, or typed normalization failures to the initiating surface/runtime seam.

STT, TTS, and vision-capable model routing are service-owned, not connector-owned. Camera vision proof captures through internal/camera and then submits canonical camera_frame parts through the normal pipeline rather than using a direct LLM shortcut.
The model/service boundary returns media-derived text, route attribution, latency, or typed provider/backend failures.
Media becomes canonical message parts with metadata, permission, storage reference, and retention state.
The pipeline returns final assistant content or framework-originated terminal failure before optional media egress.

After the canonical assistant response exists, shared media egress prepares optional TTS audio for capable surfaces. Surfaces transport those bytes or render explicit fallback text; they do not choose TTS policy or synthesize locally.

guarantees: Connectors remain transport-only for media.
Transport surfaces may attach media bytes or references but do not interpret the media.
Transport surfaces do not normalize compressed audio for STT; that conversion is owned by the runtime voice service.
Transport surfaces may deliver runtime-prepared response audio but do not synthesize it.
Users can identify and select the intended media devices before capture or playback.
Full voice is not complete until bidirectional audible conversation works.
Vision is not complete until a real captured frame grounds a response or action.
failureBehavior: Unavailable devices, denied permissions, and missing backends produce typed operator-visible failures.
knownGaps: Live media UX is a strict v1.1.0 release gate.

flows.name: Mail receive and send
flows.summary: inbound mail, inbound mail, and outbound mail operations use standard mail protocols, allowing Proton Bridge to work as local inbound mail/outbound mail without special provider logic.
flows.trigger: Operator configures or requests mail integration.

steps.summary: Mail credentials and endpoints are configured explicitly through local config/keystore paths.
Daemon startup and first-party smoke commands use the same runtime-owned translation from channels.email config plus keystore credentials into mail service configuration.
The mail service returns configured, enabled, reachable, credential-proof, or typed failure state.

Mail content is fetched via inbound mail or inbound mail and normalized for channel/runtime processing. Daemon inbound mail polling searches unread mail; release smoke can explicitly search all inbound mail messages with a subject filter to prove existing test mail without depending on read state.
Adapters return normalized mail messages, skipped-message counts, and typed receive failures.

The agent's read_inbox tool calls ListRecent to fetch recent messages (last N days, BODY.PEEK so mail is never marked seen, bounded bodies) for summarization. This is a direct read of the configured mailbox, not inbound channel intake, and does not apply the sender allowlist.
Returns from/subject/date and bounded full bodies for the agent to summarize; verified live against a Proton Bridge.
outbound mail send operations use configured endpoints and record typed outcome/failure state.
The operator or smoke harness records delivery evidence or the service records a typed send failure.

guarantees: Proton Bridge support is achieved through standard local inbound mail/outbound mail configuration.

Mail sender allowlists are optional mailbox filters. Empty mail sender allowlists accept mailbox-delivered mail; populated allowlists compare normalized From email addresses instead of raw display headers.
Mail remains critical data with secret redaction and explicit setup.
failureBehavior: Auth, connection, parse, and send failures are typed and surfaced.
verification: Live inbound mail/inbound mail/outbound mail smoke
knownGaps: Live mail smoke remains a release gate.

flows.name: First-party UI state synchronization
flows.summary: Dashboard pages hydrate and update from topic snapshots/events, while local CLI/TUI use local service seams for state that is available in process.
flows.trigger: UI page navigation, refresh, reconnect, or domain event.

steps.summary: Dashboard loads page-specific topic snapshots and renders loading, empty, and error states.
The websocket control plane returns topic data, empty state, loading state, or typed errors to the dashboard.

Runtime changes should emit events where practical instead of forcing polling. Model-selection and model-shift events cause the dashboard to invalidate cached stats/routing snapshots and request fresh topic state before re-rendering prompt-performance views. Benchmark exercise writers publish stats.changed so exercise runs and scorecards refresh through the same stats topic path.
Subscribed dashboards receive domain events and can refresh the affected topic without polling REST.
Local surfaces render state from local services or fail loudly for daemon-live-only state.

guarantees: Dashboard state does not depend on direct API polling.
Snapshot-only surfaces are transitional unless data is static or manually refreshed.
Meaningless truncated IDs should be replaced by user-meaningful labels where available.
failureBehavior: Disconnected websocket state disables controls with clear user-facing language.
knownGaps: Some dashboard pages and labels remain under validation.

flows.name: FlowForge knowledge base retrieval
flows.summary: FlowForge can answer product setup, UI, CLI/TUI, integration, and troubleshooting questions from canonical source docs and source-derived inventories rather than copied semantic-memory facts when the agent chooses the read-only product-knowledge tool.
flows.trigger: A user asks how FlowForge works, how to configure a feature, where a UI control lives, why an operation failed, or what a release gate means.

steps.summary: The pipeline exposes a read-only tool; the agent invokes it only when the whole request asks for FlowForge product facts. Greetings and ordinary conversation continue through normal inference.
The service uses a generated index/cache only when source hashes prove it is current; the index is disposable derived data.
The store returns index freshness, source hashes, and coverage diagnostics.
Installed runtimes load the registry-managed product-knowledge source snapshot from the FlowForge home; development runtimes may fall back to the source checkout.
Configuration returns the installed source snapshot path or a typed missing/stale source state.
Retrieved context includes source path/title provenance and explicit gaps when docs are missing or stale.
The pipeline receives cited facts or a source-coverage gap and uses that as bounded answer evidence.
CLI command knowledge is generated from flowforge --help and subcommand help; weak help output is fixed at the CLI source instead of copied into knowledge data.
The executable help surface returns current command inventory or exposes help-quality gaps to fix at source.
The final answer distinguishes documented product behavior from live runtime configuration, access, and health state.

guarantees: Canonical docs and source-derived inventories remain the authority.
Generated indexes are rebuildable caches, not durable facts.
Live access claims require runtime evidence, not documentation text.
Product knowledge is not a pre-LLM shortcut and must not hijack ordinary conversation.
failureBehavior: Missing or stale sources produce explicit knowledge-gap responses and release-gate findings.
knownGaps: Manifest validation, query-time source search, and tool wiring exist; installed-runtime documentation distribution remains under validation.

flows.name: Release validation
flows.summary: Release readiness is proven through docs, architecture audit, unit/lint/race tests, smoke, soaks, benchmarks, manual drills, and release notes/site collateral.
flows.trigger: Release branch approaches review or ceremony readiness.

steps.summary: Unit, integration, race, lint, and architecture tests run without touching production state.
Test execution returns pass/fail evidence, isolated state paths, and typed failures.
Behavioral soaks generate quantitative and qualitative scorecards against intentional model routes.
The pipeline/harness returns scenario outcomes, scorecards, route attribution, log findings, and stress diagnostics.
Voice, camera, mail, Windows repair, Web UI, CLI, and TUI manual drills record commit, OS, route, config root, and evidence path.
Windows stale-sidecar repair and platform service-manager checks run through the release harness instead of ad hoc manual cleanup.
The platform service boundary returns repair success, blocker, or typed manual-action evidence.
The harness reports pass, blocked, or failed manual evidence with the exact evidence path.
Final successful behavior-soak scorecards and release evidence are copied into release notes before ceremony.
Release collateral generation returns publication status or a typed release blocker.

guarantees: Testing never mutates production ~/.flowforge-dev
Release ceremony cannot start until strict release gates are green.
Full local and the cloud provider behavior soaks are separate evidence.
failureBehavior: Any failed gate blocks release rather than being scoped down silently.
knownGaps: v1.1.0 closure plan still has open manual and implementation gates.
